audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Exodus / Shemot text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for final plague announcement, favor, firstborn judgment, distinction, and Moses' anger.

verses.restored: YHWH said to Moses, One more plague I will bring upon Pharaoh and upon Egypt; after this he will send you from here. When he sends, driving, he will drive you all from here.
Speak now in ears of the people, and let each man ask from his companion and each woman from her companion objects of silver and objects of gold.
YHWH gave the people favor in eyes of Egypt. Also the man Moses was very great in land of Egypt, in eyes of Pharaoh's servants and in eyes of the people.
Moses said, Thus says YHWH: About midnight I am going out in midst of Egypt.
Every firstborn in land of Egypt shall die, from firstborn of Pharaoh sitting on his throne to firstborn of female servant who is behind the millstones, and every firstborn of beast.
There shall be a great cry in all land of Egypt, such as there has not been and such as will not be again.
But against all children of Israel, not a dog shall sharpen its tongue, against human or beast, so that you may know YHWH makes distinction between Egypt and Israel.
All these your servants shall come down to me and bow to me, saying, Go out, you and all the people at your feet; and after that I will go out. And he went out from Pharaoh in burning anger.
YHWH said to Moses, Pharaoh will not listen to you, so that my wonders may multiply in land of Egypt.
Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh, and YHWH strengthened/hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he did not send the children of Israel from his land.

translation_notes: This chapter announces the firstborn judgment in direct reversal of Pharaoh's firstborn death policy from Exodus 1.
Favor in eyes of Egypt complicates ethnic reading: Egyptians can regard Israel/Moses favorably even while Pharaoh resists.
Moses' burning anger is preserved as moral outrage before systemic death.
safety_note: The firstborn judgment is ethically severe and must be kept within the narrative frame of Pharaoh's own death-dealing policy and repeated hardening. Do not detach it into general violence theology or anti-Egyptian contempt.
